text.title: Backlog Management Process: Capture, Groom, Prioritize, Refine, Cleanup
text.summary: Five-step backlog management process with rules and antipatterns for each step.

rule.statement: Capture from all sources (feature requests, bug reports, tech debt, research outcomes, stakeholder input, team ideas) quickly and without evaluation. Evaluate at grooming, not at intake.
rule.rationale: Evaluation at intake creates a bottleneck and discourages submission. Capture quickly; triage in batch at the weekly grooming session.

rule.statement: Run a weekly grooming session (1-2 hours): review new items, refine top items, re-prioritize based on latest learning, archive stale items. Grooming skipped once starts a degradation loop.
rule.rationale: A backlog that is not groomed weekly accumulates noise faster than signal. One missed session doubles the next session's load.

rule.statement: Apply a documented prioritization method (RICE, MoSCoW, or stack ranking) and re-run it when: new information arrives, goals change, or sprint planning begins. Never leave priority implicit.
rule.rationale: Implicit priority means the backlog reverts to insertion order or loudest-voice order at sprint planning.

rule.statement: For top-of-backlog items: break into stories no larger than one sprint, add Given/When/Then acceptance criteria, estimate, and resolve all blockers. An item is Ready only when it meets all four criteria.
rule.rationale: Items missing any of the four criteria will cause sprint planning delays or mid-sprint scope discovery.

antipattern.description: Acceptance criteria pasted verbatim from feature requests or support tickets.
antipattern.reason: Customer-sourced AC is typically ambiguous prose, not testable Given/When/Then statements. Unreviewed AC causes failed sprint review.

rule.statement: Archive items with 180+ days of inactivity (unless priority is P1). Merge items with title similarity above 0.85 after human review. Delete confirmed duplicates. Target: stale ratio below 10%.
rule.rationale: Stale items degrade signal-to-noise and make backlog size metrics meaningless. The 180-day rule provides an objective threshold that avoids "nobody dares delete" paralysis.

rule.statement: Treat auto-archive as a proposed status, not direct deletion. Archive proposal requires human approval before any item with linked PRs or customer commitments is removed.
rule.rationale: Auto-archive of items with external commitments creates support and trust issues. Human approval on the archive list is the safety gate.
